summary: In this autobiographical excerpt from Ecce Homo, Friedrich Nietzsche reflects on his life, his philosophical mission, and the origins of his unique perspective. He outlines his rejection of traditional ideals, his views on sickness and health as dual lenses for evaluating values, and his personal rules for intellectual warfare and self-preservation.

principles.statement: I do not set up any new idols; my business is to overthrow them.
principles.rationale: When an ideal world is falsely assumed, reality is robbed of its value, meaning, and truthfulness. The lie of the ideal makes mankind's instincts mendacious.
principles.source_quote: To overthrow idols (idols is the name I give to all ideals) is much more like my business. In proportion as an ideal world has been falsely assumed, reality has been robbed of its value, its meaning, and its truthfulness.

principles.statement: Error is not a result of blindness, but of cowardice.
principles.rationale: Every step forward in knowledge requires courage, hardness towards oneself, and cleanliness towards oneself. People cling to ideals out of fear of the truth.
principles.source_quote: Error (the belief in the ideal) is not blindness; error is cowardice.... Every conquest, every step forward in knowledge, is the outcome of courage, of hardness towards one's self, of cleanliness towards one's self.

principles.statement: Illness can be a powerful stimulus to life, provided one is intrinsically sound.
principles.rationale: A fundamentally healthy nature uses illness to discover life afresh, instinctively choosing remedies that turn serious accidents to its own advantage.
principles.source_quote: An intrinsically morbid nature cannot become healthy. On the other hand, to an intrinsically sound nature, illness may even constitute a powerful stimulus to life, to a surplus of life.

principles.statement: One must regard oneself as a destiny and not wish to be different.
principles.rationale: Accepting one's fate prevents the exhaustion of resentment and the futile desire to change the unchangeable.
principles.source_quote: To regard one's self as a destiny, not to wish one's self "different"—this, in such circumstances, is sagacity, itself.

frameworks.name: War Tactics
frameworks.when_to_apply: When choosing opponents, engaging in intellectual or philosophical warfare, or deciding what to attack.
steps: Attack only things that are triumphant; if necessary, wait until they become triumphant.
Attack only those things against which you stand alone and find no allies, compromising nobody but yourself.
Never make personal attacks; use a personality merely as a magnifying-glass to render a general, elusive evil more apparent.
Attack only those things from which all personal differences and background disagreeable experiences are excluded.
frameworks.source_quote: My war tactics can be reduced to four principles: First, I attack only things that are triumphant... Secondly, I attack only those things against which I find no allies... Thirdly, I never make personal attacks... Fourthly, I attack only those things from which all personal differences are excluded

mental_models.name: Russian Fatalism
mental_models.description: A survival mechanism of complete non-reaction adopted when conditions are unbearable, used to conserve vital energy and prevent the rapid exhaustion caused by resentment.
mental_models.example: Holding tenaciously to insufferable conditions, places, and companions for years without revolting against them, simply because reacting would consume too much energy during a period of severe illness.

mental_models.name: Philosophy as Mountain Climbing
mental_models.description: Viewing the pursuit of truth as a voluntary retirement into harsh, lonely, but bracing high-altitude environments where one seeks out the strange and questionable.
mental_models.example: Breathing the "air of the heights" in Zarathustra, where the ice is near and the loneliness is terrible, but one can breathe freely and see everything lying beneath.

mental_models.name: Reversing Perspectives
mental_models.description: The ability to look upon healthy concepts from the standpoint of the sick, and conversely, to look down upon the instincts of decadence from the standpoint of rich, self-reliant life.
mental_models.example: Using the experience of extreme physiological weakness to understand decadence, and then using the instinct of self-recovery to evaluate those decadent values.

heuristics: I do not refute ideals; all I do is to draw on my gloves in their presence.
To be one's enemy's equal—this is the first condition of an honourable duel.
Where one despises, one cannot wage war.
I dispatch a pot of jam in order to get rid of a bitter experience.
That which does not kill him makes him stronger.

signature_quotes.text: Philosophy, as I have understood it hitherto, is a voluntary retirement into regions of ice and mountain-peaks—the seeking—out of everything strange and questionable in existence, everything upon which, hitherto, morality has set its ban.
signature_quotes.context: Describing his definition of philosophy and the bracing, lonely nature of his work.

signature_quotes.text: Error (the belief in the ideal) is not blindness; error is cowardice.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining that people cling to ideals because they lack the courage to face reality.

signature_quotes.text: To regard one's self as a destiny, not to wish one's self "different"—this, in such circumstances, is sagacity, itself.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing how he survived his lowest ebb of illness by adopting fatalism and rejecting resentment.

signature_quotes.text: He knows how to turn serious accidents to his own advantage; that which does not kill him makes him stronger.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the signs of 'Nature's lucky strokes' (a fundamentally sound and healthy person).

signature_quotes.text: The knight of knowledge must be able not only to love his enemies, but also to hate his friends.
signature_quotes.context: Quoting his own work, Zarathustra, on the necessity of independence for a seeker of truth.

anti_patterns.description: Indulging in resentment, mortification, and the thirst for revenge.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It involves a rapid wasting away of nervous energy and an abnormal increase of detrimental secretions. Nothing consumes an exhausted or sick man more quickly.
It is a sign of weakness and an inability to withstand a stimulus. It lacks reverence and distance, often thrusting fatally destructive hands into a great destiny or lonely retirement.

anti_patterns.description: Keeping silent and swallowing a grievance when offended.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Silence is an objection that produces a bad temper and upsets the stomach. It lacks subtlety and refinement of heart, making people dyspeptic, whereas rudeness is more straightforward and humane.
